text: laparoscopic primary repair of perforated diverticulitis by Dr. Andrew Iraniha. This is a fifty-two-year-old female who presented with two days history of severe left lower quadrant abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting and fever and found to have sigmoid diverticulitis with microperforation on her CT scan. Despite conservative therapy with bowel rest and antibiotics, she continued to have increasing abdominal pain and tenderness. She then underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy and found to have sigmoid diverticulitis with intraabdominal abscess. and no gross fecal contamination.

The laparoscopic view reveals significant sigmoid diverticulitis with pericolonic inflammatory adhesions and a small interloop abscess. The surgeon drains the intra-abdominal purulent fluid and irrigates the abdomen with an antibiotic solution, addressing the microperforation of the sigmoid colon without gross fecal contamination.
The surgeon performs a primary repair of the perforated diverticulitis using multiple intracorporeal sutures with a two-hole etabond technique, aiming to avoid the need for a temporary colostomy.

This patient tolerated the procedure very well and was discharged home on day number three after the operation with two antibiotics. Three months later, she underwent a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis. This approach did eliminate the requirement for creation of a temporary colostomy and improved the patient's quality of life tremendously within that three months interval. Obviously, this surgical option would not be suitable in most emergency cases when there's gross contamination or when patients are extremely sick and unstable. However, in selected cases, it could be the alternative surgical option.
